titleStmt.title: Letter from Mr. McDonagh to the Under-Secretary, Dublin Castle, 22 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Mr McDonagh

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Thomas McDonagh to Sir Matthew Nathan to ask for something to be done regarding the selling of turf and peat fuel at higher prices. McDonagh requests that all fuel be commandeered by the authorities or further quantities of coal to be delivered to the Donegal area to prevent traders profiteering.Thomas McDonagh was an ex-detective sergeant of the Royal Irish Constabulary in Donegal. In the 1911 Census Return Thomas McDonagh is reported as being 53 years old -- a widower with two daughters. Sir Matthew Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Buncrana Donegal 22. 4. 16 Under Secty Dublin Castle. Sir Can nothing be done
to stop the extortion in the
price of Peat Fuel in this
locality. An ordinary cart
holds about 2 cwt. in
many cases less, and
the sellers will not give it
for less than 4/3 which
is about Â£2.2 per
ton. These men run no
risk in transporting
the turf & they are
imposing on the
softness of the
military peoples
wives & daughters I suggest that all
the Fuel be commandeered
& issued as needed
or that coal be sent to
the town & issued as
the richer are hoarding
& the poor must go to
neighbours to get
food cooked. The chief offenders
in price have their
sons walking about in
illegally received boots
& shirts (military ) Thomas Mc Donagh Ex Det.. Sergt. RIC
